sections.heading: This Act is the Computer Misuse Act 1993.
sections.title: Definition of Computer Misuse Act 1993
sections.summary: This section establishes the Computer Misuse Act 1993 as the governing legislation. It defines key terms including 'computer', 'computer output', 'computer service', 'damage', 'data', and other technical terms essential for the Act's application. The definitions specify what constitutes unauthorized access, modification, and use of computer systems, and outline the scope of protected data and systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines critical terms used in data protection and cybersecurity contexts, including 'computer', 'data', and 'unauthorized access', which are foundational for understanding data transfer and system security requirements.

requirements.text: This Act is the Computer Misuse Act 1993

sections.source_quote: 1. This Act is the Computer Misuse Act 1993.

sections.heading: This Act is the Computer Misuse Act 1993. (cont.)
This Act is the Computer Misuse Act 1993. (cont.)
sections.title: Offences for obtaining or using personal information unlawfully
sections.summary: This section defines offences for obtaining, retaining, or supplying personal information obtained through unlawful means. It specifies that a person commits an offence if they knowingly or with reason to believe the information was obtained in contravention of sections 3, 4, 5, or 6, and then obtain or retain the information or supply it. The section also outlines exceptions where the person did not use the information for criminal purposes or did not know it would be used for criminal purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which personal data transfers are unlawful, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border data flows where data was obtained through unauthorized access.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if they knowingly or with reason to believe personal information was obtained in contravention of sections 3, 4, 5, or 6 and then obtain or retain the information
It is not an offence if the person obtained or retained the information for a purpose other than using it to commit or facilitate criminal offences
It is not an offence if the person did not know or have reason to believe the information would be used to commit or facilitate criminal offences
A person does not transmit personal information merely because they provide network access or services

sections.source_quote: A person shall be guilty of an offence if the person, knowing or having reason to believe that any personal information about another person (being an individual) was obtained by an act done in contravention of section 3, 4, 5 or 6 — (a) obtains or retains the personal information; or (b) supplies, offers to supply, transmits or makes available, by any means the personal information.

sections.heading: This Act is the Computer Misuse Act 1993. (cont.)
sections.title: Territorial scope of computer misuse offences
sections.summary: This section establishes that offences under the Act apply to persons outside Singapore and that offences committed outside Singapore may be treated as if committed within Singapore. It specifies conditions for applicability, including whether the accused was in Singapore, the computer was in Singapore, or the offence causes serious harm in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by defining when cross-border data misuse offences apply, relevant for data transfers involving Singaporean entities operating internationally.

requirements.text: The Act applies to persons outside Singapore
Offences committed outside Singapore may be treated as if committed within Singapore
The Act applies if the accused was in Singapore at the material time
The Act applies if the computer was in Singapore at the material time
The Act applies if the offence causes serious harm in Singapore

sections.source_quote: Subject to subsection (3), the provisions of this Act have effect, in relation to any person, whatever the person's nationality or citizenship, outside as well as within Singapore.

sections.title: Criminal Law (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act 2025
sections.summary: This section amends the Computer Misuse Act 1993 by incorporating the Act 21 of 2025—Criminal Law (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act 2025. It specifies the bill number (15/2025), first reading date (14 October 2025), second and third reading dates (4 November 2025), and commencement date (30 December 2025). The amendment focuses on cybersecurity enhancements without detailing specific obligations.

requirements.text: Bill number 15/2025
First reading date 14 October 2025
Second and third reading dates 4 November 2025
Commencement date 30 December 2025

sections.source_quote: 15.  Act 21 of 2025—Criminal Law (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act 2025 Bill: 15/2025 First Reading: 14 October 2025 Second and Third Readings: 4 November 2025 Commencement: 30 December 2025

sections.title: Abetment of scam offences
sections.summary: This section defines abetment, conspiracy, or attempt to commit offences mentioned in item 1 of the Scam Offences list, which are specific cyber-related offences under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it addresses the legal framework for handling third-party involvement in data misuse, particularly in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Any person who abets the commission of or attempts to commit an offence under this Act is guilty of that offence

sections.source_quote: An abetment of, or a conspiracy or an attempt to commit, an offence mentioned in item 1.

sections.summary: This section amends the Evidence Act 1996 through the Evidence (Amendment) Act 1996. The amendment was enacted via Bill 45/1995 and came into effect on 8 March 1996. It outlines the legislative process and commencement date for the amendment.

sections.title: Amendment of Criminal Procedure Code 2010
sections.summary: This section amends the Criminal Procedure Code 2010 through the Criminal Procedure Code 2010. The amendment was enacted via Bill 11/2010 and came into effect on 2 January 2011 (section 430 read with item 24 of the Sixth Schedule). It details the legislative process and commencement date for the amendment.

sections.heading: Act 3 of 2013—Computer Misuse (Amendment) Act 2013
sections.title: Amendment of Computer Misuse Act 2013
sections.summary: This section amends the Computer Misuse Act through the Computer Misuse (Amendment) Act 2013. The amendment was enacted via Bill 36/2012 and came into effect on 13 March 2013. It details the legislative process and commencement date for the amendment, and notes that the Computer Misuse Act was renamed as the Computer Misuse and Cybersecurity Act by this Act.

It specifies the bill number (2/2018), first reading date (8 January 2018), second and third reading dates (5 February 2018), and commencement date (31 August 2018) for section 49. The amendment renames the Computer Misuse and Cybersecurity Act as the Computer Misuse Act.
